title: RA.980 Philippa Pacho

translation.content: It's a tale as old as time: techno DJ moves to Berlin to chase a dream. Philippa Pacho is part of the latest wave of talent to tread that familiar path. But don't let that fool you. She's one of the classiest artists around—no TikTok gimmicks here. Pacho's formative years were spent in her native Stockholm, navigating a vibrant DIY party scene that included warehouse raves and illegal parties. She eventually took up a long-standing residency at beloved local club Under Bron and the experience, as she details in this week's Q&A, sharpened her technical skills, teaching her to handle every type of set—whether opening with patience, supporting a headliner or closing with finesse. She also played with countless touring DJs, from Answer Code Request to Anthony Parasole, which ultimately inspired her move to Berlin. That relocation has firmly paid off. It's testament to Pacho's talent that today you'll find her playing pretty much every big club and festival across Europe, from Berghain and Dekmantel to FOLD and Monument (we were exhausted just looking at her upcoming listings on RA). Other recent highlights include closing out Bassiani's tenth birthday, and back-to-backs with Fadi Mohem and Sandrien. Pacho's RA Podcast showcases her preference for classy, hypnotic techno, striking a balance between muscle and subtle groove. Her aptitude for what makes dance floors tick is also evident on her two labels—Phorum Records and positivesource (co-run with Blue Hour)—both of which mirror her DJ style, blending intensity with delicate textures. positivesource has released several standout records, including this year's "Psycho" by BLANKA and Phil Berg's "Psyckik" (which you'll hear on this mix). Spanning just over 70 minutes, RA.980 is a window into Pacho's thoroughly modern sound (the oldest track is from 2018). And yet it retains a refined, sweeping quality. Give it a spin and you'll soon twig why she's in demand on dance floors worldwide. —Bella Aquilina You started out DJing at illegal parties in Stockholm before becoming a regular at Under Bron. How would you describe the music scene in the capital, and how has it shaped you as an artist? I've been based in Berlin for over a decade, so I can't say much about the scene in Stockholm currently, as I haven't been immersed in it for a while. I do, however, have great memories from my early rave years. My impression is that things have changed a lot, but there used to be a great underground scene with many interesting acts and underground parties in various warehouses and in nature. Soon after, I started going out to clubs and raves, a bunch of friends and I started organising our own parties in a club called 2.35:1 that sadly no longer exists. We also organised some illegal events with a proper sound system and international acts, as the opening hours in regular venues didn’t accommodate a 12-hour party. With time, more and more girls in our crew (female-run collective) moved to different cities or started families, which led to our nights and parties fading. I started focusing more on my residency at Under Bron, which lasted for over a decade. Being at Under Bron and having access to network with other international DJs who would come to do shows was a pleasant experience, and I started realising that I needed to leave Stockholm as I craved for more; therefore, moving to Berlin was an obvious choice at the time and somewhere I was already experiencing quite often in parallel. Having a residency at a prolific club like Under Bron teaches you how to approach different time slots, how to perfect technical skills, being the first act of the night and having patience as I was often supporting a headliner. Hence, you had to warm up nicely or take over and close down the floor in a smooth way. Since the pandemic, you've been touring more frequently. How has that experience been for you—both creatively and personally? It's been fulfilling and exciting to be part of the scene in a more professional way. Meanwhile, so many changes have happened in just a short period of time. It's been talked about often, but the community who was clubbing before the pandemic undoubtedly saw changes in many forms, such as stylistically, but also, social media has played a more significant role, much like any industry. It used to feel more underground before the techno hype exploded, but probably that's also giving me opportunities, so I can't complain too much. It was also reasonably common before that artists, parties, or collectives were more secretive to maintain an element of mystery, but that approach became a bit overplayed and seems long gone. The market now is competitive, so you kind of have to put yourself out there. On the other hand, inclusivity has been brought forward and prioritised. It's incredibly refreshing to see because having an all-male lineup was pretty much the norm for a long time. Your label and DJ sets often explore the Detroit-influenced shades of techno. What draws you to that sound, and what qualities do you look for in the music you play or release? I'm drawn to raw, deep, textured-leaning music that takes the mind somewhere. I think all music should have a story to tell or take you to a new weird or exciting place. These things act as my blueprint while digging for music and DJing. Although the first record on my label only came out towards the end of last year, these are also the aspects that will continue to form the foundation for my label, Phorum Records. With postivesource, my label partner Luke (Blue Hour) and I are more playful and open to different sounds, experimenting and taking risks. What have you been working on recently? I've been working on the forthcoming releases for Phorum Records and positivesource and look forward to sharing more news on that soon. Additionally, I've recently joined a bigger agency (Triangle), so I'm working closely with my agent on my calendar, filling key events and festival dates, including some exciting back-to-backs. Things have grown significantly over the past few years, and I'm happy to embrace facing future challenges and creating new experiences. How and where was your recent mix recorded? What was the inspiration behind it? I recorded the mix in one take at home using three Pioneer CDJ3000s and Xone:96 Allen and Heath mixer. The inspiration came from many memorable club experiences over the past few months! It's a collection of what I would normally play at a peak-time slot. What are you most looking forward to in the near future—whether musically or otherwise? I have quite a lot of gigs coming up, including some nice festivals such as Dekmantel, Glitch and Stone Techno and at my favourite clubs such as BASEMENT, FOLD and Berghain. I'm also really looking forward to building my label and releasing more music. Besides that, I'd like to find more time to spend with my family and have time off. As much as I'm grateful for what I get to do, it's also very intense and can sometimes feel like a relentless bubble, so it's good to take a step away from time to time. Is there a social or political cause that's particularly important to you right now? So many problems and injustices are happening worldwide, regardless of continent, background, culture, religion or class. Things don't feel positive right now, and it's hard to stay hopeful when you see how societies and people struggle for their important causes. Times are scary and unfair, and I'm not feeling optimistic, to be honest. It's okay to point that out. I find it hard to live peacefully and freely when so many people are not, especially when you read from reliable sources that political votes are being stolen, voices are being silenced or people are being cancelled for whatever reason. Can you tell us about a club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I find it hard to accept myself as an artist. I collect music from others and play it in clubs, but I do try my best to capture the moment and do it creatively, taking people on a journey. Whether others think that's artistic or not is up to them, but I’m personally not 100 percent comfortable with that term. The club that has inspired me the most is probably not a big shocker to anyone: Berghain. I've been going there since I started clubbing at 19 years old. I've had the best (and some not-so-great) nights there. Several years ago, I could never imagine playing there as it was just too farfetched of an idea for me, even if I was already DJing. However, here I am playing there regularly, and it's still quite a surreal feeling to be on the other side and not only on the dance floor. Another source of inspiration is, without a doubt, Georgia; I still cannot believe that ten years ago, they basically didn't have the club infrastructure that they have today and now it's one of the best in the world, if not the best. The freedom and euphoria on a Georgian dance floor, no matter where you are (Tbilisi, Kutaisi, Batumi, etc), is something I seldom experience in European clubs, or it's just different and its own thing entirely. This innocence and deep curiosity among the Georgian crowd make every trip there worthwhile.
translation.blurb: Modern hypnotics from a new techno star.
